Long ago, before the sky had texture, the heavens were a vast, empty blue dome. It was monotonous and still, offering no shade to the world below. High atop the jagged, unclimbable peak of Mount Aethelgard lived a solitary weaver named Elara. Elara was terribly lonely, for the mountain was too steep for any mortal visitor.

One day, the West Wind, pitying her solitude, brought her a flock of celestial sheep. These were no ordinary beasts; their fleece was made of raw mist and gathered starlight. Elara, delighted by their company, began to shear them and spin the vaporous wool upon her great wooden wheel.

She knit vast, fluffy blankets of white to keep the cold mountain warm. But the wind, playful and mischievous, would snatch her creations and scatter them across the sky. At first, Elara wept, her tears falling to the earth as the first rain. But when she looked up, she saw her blankets drifting as majestic white ships sailing the blue sea above.

Now, Elara weaves endlessly. On calm days, she spins fine, wispy strands that stretch like silk. When she is angry, she spins thick, dark knots of gray wool that block the sun. And when she accidentally drops her heavy spindle on the floor, the world below hears the crash of thunder.

Overnight, a stalk of pure emerald shot upward. It was the First Bamboo. Unlike the sideways trees, it possessed a stubborn, rigid spine. It pushed against the grey slate, groaning under the pressure. Elara watered it with her tears of frustration, and the bamboo grew thicker, faster, and stronger.

With a mighty crack that echoed across the world, the bamboo pierced the slate. It surged upward, lifting the heavy sky on its shoulders. As the sky rose, light flooded in, painting the grey slate into a brilliant blue.
